title: Hypothalamus-Pituitary Axis
body_html:
The hypothalamus is a division of the diencephalon that is principally involved in homeostasis.

Anatomically it is located in close proximity to the hypothalamus with which it is closely related. The hypothalamus secretes a number of releasing hormones which trigger the release of hormones from the anterior pituitary. The hormones of the posterior pituitary (ADH and oxytocin) are actually synthesised in the hypothalamus then transported to the posterior pituitary and stored pending release.

Pituitary gland and relation to hypothalamus and optic chiasm. click on image to enlarge

Hypothalamus-Pituitary axis. *note the posterior pituitary hormones are synthesised in the hypothalamus itself so do not require releasing hormone stimulation. Click on image to enlarge

Disorders of pituitary function
Pituitary disorders may be broadly categorised into deficiency disorders related to underproduction of pituitary hormones or excess production of pituitary hormones. Pituitary tumours may cause over or underproduction of pituitary hormones. Head injuries, autoimmune conditions, vascular disease and infection are other causes of pituitary dysfunction.

HormoneDeficiencyExcess
ADHCentral (cranial) diabetes insipidusSIADH
OxytocinDepression symptomsEmotional lability
FSH and LHHypogonadism
Infertility
Failure of secondary sexual characteristic development
Low testosterone causing low muscle mass and bone density		Infertility
Ovarian hyperstimulation
Oligomenorrhea
Cystic ovaries
TSHSecondary hypothyroidismSecondary hyperthyroidism
ProlactinInsufficient lactation
Subfertility
Delayed puberty
Menstrual disorders		Anovulatory infertility,
Amenorrhea or oligomenorrhea
Galactorrhea (lactation not associated with pregnancy)
Growth hormonePituitary dwarfism in children
Decreased muscle mass
Decreased bone density
Increased central adipose tissue
Increased LDL to HDL cholesterol ratio
Tiredness and depression		Acromegaly in adults or gigantism in children
CortisolAdrenal insufficiencyCushing syndrome


Dopamine
Dopamine is a hormone released by several areas of the brain, most notably the substantia nigra, ventral tegmental area and the hypothalamus.

Dopamine has many roles including pain modulation, neuronal functioning, arousal and sexual gratification. It is also an inhibitor of prolactin.

Melanocyte-stimulating hormones (MSH)
MSH is produced by the anterior pituitary. MSH and ACTH share the same precursor molecule (proopiomelanocortin or POMC) which is cleaved into ACTH and MSH. Conditions where ACTH is raised, such as pregnancy, Cushing disease and primary adrenal insufficiency, typically feature hyperpigmentation as a result of ACTH binding to melanocytes and raised MSH.



The nuclei of the hypothalamus
The hypothalamus is made up of groups of nuclei. There are 3 main nuclei areas:
  • Anterior (Supraoptic) nuclei
  • Middle (Tuberal) Nuclei
  • Posterior (Mammillary) Nuclei
These nuclei may be further subdivided on their anatomical position including their anatomical relationship to the optic nerve. The layout of the nuclei and an overview of their function is shown below to help understanding. A detailed knowledge is not required for the exam.


Nuclei of the hypothalamus. Click on image to enlarge


The table below shows the major nuclei of the hypothalamus:

RegionAreaNucleusFunctions
Anterior (supraoptic)PreopticPreoptic nucleusThermoregulation
Anterior (supraoptic)MedialMedial preoptic nucleusRegulates the release of gonadotropic hormones from the adenohypophysis
Contains the sexually dimorphic nucleus, which releases GnRH, differential development between sexes is based upon in utero testosterone levels
